movie.title: The Odyssey
movie.overview: Odysseus, the legendary King of Ithaca, embarks on a long and perilous journey home following the Trojan War. Throughout his voyage, he is forced to confront the whims of gods, mythological monsters, and trials that stretch both his cunning and his humanity to the breaking point.

alternativeTitles.title: มหากาพย์โอดิสซี

reviews.author: MovieGuys
reviews.content:
Monty Python couldn't improve on this. Suffice to say, someone doesn't know their classical Greek history too well. They have excavated remains from the site of Troy and undertaken facial recreations. Would it surprise anyone to know they look Greek? Would it surprise anyone, too, to know the main figures of Homer's work, are described clearly, as Greek. Oh and for anyone thinking its all a fiction, there is increasing evidence to suggest that the work is based on fact.

Upsides include reasonable acting, great sets, okay action and top locations. 

In summary, I wonder what actual Greeks make of this reimagining of their proud past? For me, what I can say is history is what it is, not what revisionists want it to be. Enough said.

reviews.author: Manuel São Bento
reviews.content:
Check out my full review @ https://www.manuelsbento.com/the-odyssey-2026-christopher-nolans-most-faithful-epic-is-also-his-most-ambitious-one/

Rating: A

THE ODYSSEY is a maximalist, deeply faithful adaptation of Homer's poem that somehow ends on a quiet, contemplative note despite everything it throws at you along the way. Completely unafraid of its own scale and scope, it's ten years of war, myth, and horror (!) woven into the story so tightly that the whole thing paradoxically feels more grounded, not less. An astonishingly stacked ensemble led by a phenomenal Matt Damon, deceptively convincing practical effects, and filmmaking craft firing on every level. Ludwig Göransson's score alone is worth the ticket. Easily one of the best theatrical experiences of the year. This is Christopher Nolan at his most alive.

reviews.author: AlphaLucifer
reviews.content: The hype was massive, the crowd was packed, but the boredom in the theatre at the end credits said it all. A 172-minute film felt like it was a 6+ hour watch. This isn’t a respectful adaptation of Homer’s epic; it’s a bloated, self-important disaster that butchers the source material, erases Greek identity, and rams woke DEI nonsense down your throat while delivering some of the worst big-name acting I’ve seen in years. Right from the opener, it was clear Nolan had zero interest in Homer’s actual text. Instead of going back to the original Greek or a faithful classic translation, he based the whole thing on Emily Wilson’s modern, feminist-slanted version, that crappy, agenda-driven reinterpretation that twists the poem’s language, downplays traditional heroism, and injects contemporary gender politics. No wonder the soul is missing. **_The Odyssey_** is the foundational poem of Western civilisation, cunning, nostos, divine meddling, human endurance, told in sweeping oral poetry. Nolan reduces it to mere nothingness. Matt Damon as Odysseus is the biggest miscast of all; he sleepwalks through it like a tired Jason Bourne in a chiton, delivering flat quips with zero charisma or bronze-age king energy. No wit, no depth, just brooding stares. Zendaya’s Athena is wooden and out of place, Tom Holland’s Telemachus is hyperactive but empty, Anne Hathaway’s Penelope feels phoned in, and Robert Pattinson’s turn is lost in the gloom. Charlize Theron as Calypso, Jon Bernthal, walked and looking like he's out of a Robin-Hood film, and John Leguizamo all feel awkwardly shoehorned in with zero chemistry or gravitas. Worst of all is Elliot Page as Sinon (replacing Achilles after backlash); he runs around like a marionette doll with stiff, unnatural movements that kill every action beat. Lupita Nyong’o as Helen of Troy (and Clytemnestra) is completely out of place and entirely misrepresents the characters. The entire main cast is miscast to hell. Bad acting across the board sinks what little soul remains. The zero Greekness is unforgivable. This is the profoundly Greek cornerstone of Western literature, the first great literary piece of our civilisation, and Hollywood turned it into a generic fantasy IP. Greece helped fund this thing with taxpayer money, yet there’s not a single Greek actor in a major role. No olive-skinned warriors, no Mediterranean fire, no authentic Mycenaean aesthetics, let alone authentic period costumes. In fact, the landscapes and costumes look like leftover Nolan dark fantasy. Even worse, they loaded it with actors in roles that directly contradict the Homeric descriptions and classical imagery of ancient Greek heroes and gods... what the hell was Travis Scott doing in this film! It is a cultural erasure at its most blatant, profiting off Greece’s heritage while replacing the people it belongs to. The premiere didn’t even happen in Athens because they were scared of the backlash. Smart move, because Greeks are rightly outraged. The woke DEI casting is peak insult. Lupita as Helen, the face that launched a thousand ships, really? Homer's text depicted her with golden hair and fair beauty, but Nolan decided to 'modernise' the text to fit into today's woke-DEI agenda. Zendaya as Athena... with her usual dull facial expressions? Elliot Page’s tiny 'man-playing-woman-playing-man' Achilles stand-in? The rainbow ensemble screams committee quotas over truth. It’s not “inclusive”; it’s dishonest revisionism that strips the story of its European roots and historical context. On top of that, Nolan’s signature grim, dark tone, all brooding shadows and moral ambiguity, feels completely wrong for this story. **The Odyssey** belongs to bright Mediterranean sun, blue seas, blue skies, and passionate Hellenic vitality, even amid tragedy. Slapping his Batman/Dunkirk gloom over it makes everything feel oppressive and mismatched, like forcing a Nordic winter onto the Aegean; indeed, he even included Viking-style ships, of course completely unrelated to the period! The music doesn't fit in any scene, and the rap at the end credits was another WTF moment. Visually watching some scenes in IMAX is impressive, but it’s an empty spectacle. Feels like there was not even any climax to the point I thought by the middle of the film, when will it end? Save your money and don't bother. Reread the original **Odyssey** instead, **Homer's Odyssey**. In fact, go ahead and reread **Homer's Iliad** too while you're at it to see how much the actors and Nolan himself knew nothing of the actual original material. Nolan’s _The Odyssey_ is a one-star, two-star mess at best, with terrible acting, cultural disrespect, reliance on a politicised translation, 'modernised' like Nolan himself said, for the public, while in truth it was for the critics and Oscar voters, and it is also total proof of everything that is wrong with modern Hollywood, wrapped in expensive packaging.

reviews.author: jwtx
reviews.content: This has to be the best movie theater experience I’ve ever had. I unfortunately couldn’t see it in 70MM IMAX as Houston doesn’t have one and didn’t want to drive to San Antonio. I watched it in standard IMAX at Regal Lonestar 12 minutes from home. I’m a Nolan fan but not everything he’s done has been great. The passing in this movie is amazing. I was waiting for a slow part in the movie to go the bathroom but it just kept going and kept you hooked. I’ve seen all the negative reactions of this film from stupid people making stuff up without watching it. I’m in a smaller area north of Houston and a lot of us aren’t liberal but also not crazy right like the small vocal idiots are. Homers Epic was literally Epic. Nolan out did himself on this one.

reviews.author: mojo_kensa
reviews.content: ABSOLUTE trash of a movie, goes against all ethos and morals in filming, it takes way identity and places people of new world order agenda. Very poor adaptation of homer. Matt with his daughters and wife should spend time in India for the next pandemic, instead of Ireland. I will say this, pacing, story telling, amazing scenery, all of that dont fool the millenials audience, maybe genz, since they are so much into hype and 4k - its easy to mislead them. Also, lets take a moment to dedicate how far the studio has gone to show this film early in the most populated country, to get positive reviews from a very ill informed audience!

reviews.author: Brent Marchant
reviews.content: As one of the 2026 summer movie season’s most anticipated releases, writer-director Christopher Nolan’s screen adaptation of Homer’s literary epic, The Odyssey, has generated more than its share of reactions and for a variety of reasons. So I’ll come right to the point on my view of this release: I liked it, but I didn’t love it. This retelling of the diverse experiences of Greek warrior Odysseus (Matt Damon) on returning to his Grecian home of Ithaca after fighting in the Trojan War explores the many adventures and profound soul-searching of his 10-year journey, the second half of a 20-year-long absence. Waiting for him back home are his devoted wife, Penelope (Anne Hathaway), who has long been courted by a band of would-be suitors like Antinous (Robert Pattinson) who believe her husband is dead, and Telemachus (Tom Holland), the son who’s never met his father and stands to succeed him as King of Ithaca should he be officially declared deceased. This historic drama, a common reading selection in many high school English lit classes, thus follows the intrigue and adventures from the perspectives of the trip and the homefront, many of which viewers are likely to find familiar in this cinematic depiction. However, not everything works in this adaptation. For starters, the film is too long with its 2:52:00 runtime, especially during the picture’s languishing, talky opening hour. Then there’s the writing, which incorporates more than its share of ill-fitting contemporary dialogue, material that should have never made its way into the final script. And there are noticeable exclusions and underdeveloped sequences from the source material that could have been included and/or tweaked in the film narrative with the removal of overlong, extraneous material. For example, despite frequent references to the Greek gods, their presence in the film is largely absent save for a few conversations between Odysseus and Athena, the Goddess of Wisdom (Zendaya). A similar fate befalls Helen of Troy (Lupita Nyong’o), “the woman who launched a thousand ships” and prompted the Trojan War in the first place, and Calypso (Charlize Theron), the mysterious caregiver who loves an ailing Odysseus and nurses him back to health for seven years in the midst of his journey. With those shortcomings aside, however, “The Odyssey” has its share of strengths, too, such as the picture’s decidedly engaging second half, as well as its fine performances, especially those of Hathaway, Pattinson, Holland and Samantha Morton as Circe, a witch whom Odysseus encounters during the trip home. Those looking for action-adventure segments will find plenty of those here as well, although the special effects depicting them are noticeably inconsistent – great in sequences like the Greeks’ ambush of their opponent’s stronghold with the ploy of the Trojan Horse and the soldiers’ dangerous encounter with the massive, menacing whirlpool Charybdis, and not so great during their contacts with the seductively voiced Sirens, the one-eyed giant Cyclops and the multi-headed cliff-dwelling monster Scylla. It’s difficult to pinpoint exactly how and why this production failed to live up to some of the high expectations that had been set for it, though it’s a safe bet that it might have fallen prey to believing its own pre-release hype, especially coming off of the impressive and well-earned success of Nolan’s previous effort, “Oppenheimer” (2023). Obviously, viewers will have to make up their own minds, but keeping the foregoing impressions in mind may prove helpful in formulating those opinions as audiences wend their way through their own personal odysseys.

reviews.author: Nick
reviews.content: Homer's epic has endured for thousands of years, and Nolan's adaptation reminds you exactly why. The dialogue is lean and purposeful, he trusts the audience completely, and every setpiece — Cyclops, Sirens, Ithaca's reckoning — builds to a stunning finish. Superb acting, cinematography, and seeing it in IMAX just adds to everything that makes it epic. The year's best film, full stop.

reviews.author: CinemaSerf
reviews.content: With a production design worthy of Sir Ridley Scott, Christopher Nolan takes us into antiquity for his take on the second instalment of Homer's Trojan War stories. I have to say it's maybe not the most impressive of wooden horses that is left as a tribute for the maybe just a little too gullible Trojans, but now we know that this decade-long siege has all ended in a mighty conflagration and that Odysseus (Matt Damon) is keen on returning to Ithaca where his wife and son await him. On that island, though, strife is rife. Penelope (Anne Hathaway) is being besieged by an army of suitors like Antinous (Robert Pattinson) and the young Telemachus (Tom Holland) isn't really best equipped to step in and protect his mother from their machinations. Now flitting between the island's domestic issues and the legendary trials of the returning husband, Nolan tries to weave the two threads together in quite a compelling fashion. The snag for me throughout this epic drama is that we spent too much time on Ithaca with the family issues and not enough time with Odysseus and his crew as they faced a plethora of deadly foes and evil temptations as they traversed the Mediterranean. Too many of their adventures are too tightly compressed and involve a few scenes of menace before everyone rushes back to their ships - usually fewer than arrived - before continuing on their dangerous travels. Much is told thanks to his memory-provoking conversation with the lotus peddling Calypso (Charlize Theron) but again, we don't really get to grips much with who she is or what he is doing on her beautiful island. Charybdis and the Sirens, even the Cyclops are all given a little too much short shrift as we dwell more at the other end of his voyage on the issues facing the young Telemachus as he travels to Sparta to find news from Menelaus and his now restored wife Helen. The visual effects are pretty spectacular and Damon manages quite well to craft a character who is brave and ingenious but also troubled by both the destruction of Troy and by his behaviour whilst participating in that. What we also avoid here is much involvement from the Olympians. Barring some regular references to Zeus's laws of hospitality and a few scenes with Athena (Zendaya) who appears to be acting more as his psychiatrist than a goddess of war and wisdom whose statue was unceremoniously decapitated during the assault of the city, and whose temple was unceremoniously defiled - we don't really involve them. If you've read even the briefest of adaptations of the novel, you'll know that many of his difficulties getting home were due to the anger of gods like Poseidon and Apollo - yet those characters remain somewhat omni-absent. Perhaps, and I don't write this lightly, three hours simply isn't long enough to do justice to the characterful complexities of the story? I did enjoy this and it flew past, but I really wanted much more of the fantastic, the horror and the terror and maybe just a little less of the troubled souls presented here, and I was just a little bit disappointed as I left. Perhaps he's saving more for the director's cut?

reviews.author: threestardave
reviews.content:
An amazingly ambitious effort that mostly pays off. Solid acting, great production values, and an effective reuse by Nolan of plot elements and characters from Homer's epic to tell a story for the modern audience. I have no regrets having paid for theater tickets to have seen this.

A more detailed look here: https://hill-kleerup.org/blog/2026/07/19/movie-review-the-odyssey-2026.html

reviews.author: Perth Bogan
reviews.content:
Laughably bad. The funniest part, albeit unintentional, was when travis scott did his black 'slam poetry' attempt. I heard several people in the audience start giggling. It was the part of the film that I'll always remember. I bet a thousand memes will live on in history thanks to this POS movie.

Acting was bad except for Pattinson. I truly don't understand why some of these people were cast, they have zero connection to Greece. Anyway, like I said several parts were laugh out loud funny, but I'm 100% sure the director didn't mean for ppl to laugh. Next time we won't be so quick to buy a ticket to a nolan movie, probably skip the next one and maybe see it at home.

reviews.author: sweety1246
reviews.content:
only One word " Masterpiece " 
every department at its finest 
Direction top notch 
screenplay top notch 
Music and Background Tracks Top notch 
cinematography top notch 
totally a typical Nolan's film

aiEnrichment.summary_one_sentence: After the Trojan War, King Odysseus of Ithaca endures a long, perilous voyage home filled with gods, monsters, and trials that test his wit and humanity.
aiEnrichment.summary_short: The Odyssey follows Odysseus, the King of Ithaca, as he attempts to return home after the Trojan War. His journey is fraught with danger as he encounters vengeful gods, mythical creatures, and seemingly impossible challenges. Along the way, his cleverness and resilience are pushed to their limits. The story is a timeless tale of adventure, survival, and the enduring bond between a man and his homeland.
aiEnrichment.summary_long: Odysseus, the celebrated King of Ithaca, sets sail for home after the fall of Troy, eager to reunite with his wife Penelope and son Telemachus. However, the god Poseidon, angered by Odysseus's blinding of his son the Cyclops Polyphemus, curses him with endless misfortune at sea. Odysseus and his crew first face the Lotus-Eaters, whose fruit makes men forget their homes, and later escape the deadly Cyclops cave through cunning. The sorceress Circe turns his men into pigs before becoming an ally, and the journey continues past the seductive Sirens and the monstrous Scylla and Charybdis. Odysseus loses his entire crew to divine wrath and his own men's folly, eventually washing ashore alone on the island of the nymph Calypso, who holds him captive for years. Meanwhile in Ithaca, suitors overrun his palace, pressuring Penelope to remarry and plotting against young Telemachus. With the help of the goddess Athena, Telemachus seeks news of his father, and Odysseus is finally released to make his way home. Disguised as a beggar, he reveals himself only to loyal servants, slaughters the suitors, and reclaims his throne and family. The epic closes with peace restored under the watchful favor of the gods.
aiEnrichment.synopsis: The Odyssey is a sweeping fantasy-adventure drama based on the ancient Greek epic. It chronicles the arduous journey of Odysseus, King of Ithaca, as he strives to return home in the aftermath of the Trojan War. Beset by the anger of gods and the perils of mythical realms, he must rely on his intellect and endurance to survive. The narrative weaves together action, wonder, and human drama, exploring themes of loyalty, perseverance, and the longing for home.

story_structure.climax: Odysseus finally reaches Ithaca in disguise and must confront the usurpers in his household, proving his identity and reclaiming his place through a decisive confrontation.
story_structure.conflict: The gods, notably Poseidon, oppose Odysseus's return, and he becomes stranded far from Ithaca while supernatural forces and his own men's flaws create constant peril.
story_structure.resolution: Order is restored in Ithaca as Odysseus reunites with his family and reestablishes his rule, though the aftermath of his journey leaves lasting marks on him and his kingdom.
story_structure.introduction: Odysseus, King of Ithaca, departs from Troy after the conclusion of the Trojan War, setting out for his homeland with his crew, unaware of the long separation that awaits him and his family.
story_structure.risingAction: Odysseus encounters a series of mythical beings and divine trials—from enchantresses to monsters to hostile peoples—each testing his wit and endurance while his home faces invasion by suitors courting his wife.

characters.name: Odysseus
characters.description: King of Ithaca and protagonist who uses cunning and resilience to survive trials on his journey home from the Trojan War.

characters.name: Penelope
characters.description: Faithful wife of Odysseus who awaits his return in Ithaca and represents his motivation to come home.

characters.name: Telemachus
characters.description: Son of Odysseus and Penelope who matures and seeks news of his father during the hero's absence.

characters.name: Athena
characters.description: Goddess who guides and protects Odysseus and Telemachus throughout the journey with wisdom and intervention.

characters.name: Poseidon
characters.description: God of the sea who wrathfully hinders Odysseus's voyage home after the hero blinds his son Polyphemus.

characters.name: Circe
characters.description: Sorceress who initially imprisons Odysseus's men but later aids him after he resists her magic.

characters.name: Calypso
characters.description: Nymph who detains Odysseus on her island for years, offering immortality, before releasing him by order of the gods.

characters.name: Polyphemus
characters.description: Cyclops and son of Poseidon who eats Odysseus's men and is blinded by the hero to enable escape.

characters.name: Eumaeus
characters.description: Loyal swineherd who aids the disguised Odysseus upon his return to Ithaca.

characters.name: The Suitors
characters.description: Group of men who court Penelope and consume Odysseus's wealth, ultimately slain by the returning king.

relationships.description: Captor/Helper (witch who detains then aids him)
Goddess/Guide (she aids his growth and search)

faq.question: What is the title of this work?

faq.answer: Odysseus, the legendary King of Ithaca, embarks on a long and perilous journey home following the Trojan War. Throughout his voyage, he is forced to confront the whims of gods, mythological monsters, and trials that stretch both his cunning and his humanity to the breaking point.
faq.question: What is the overview of The Odyssey?

faq.answer: The Odyssey is an ancient epic poem traditionally attributed to Homer and is part of the broader Trojan War narrative, often linked with The Iliad, but it is not a modern cinematic franchise.
faq.question: Is this movie part of a franchise?

faq.answer: As a classical epic poem, The Odyssey does not have a post-credit scene.
faq.question: Is there a post-credit scene?

faq.answer: The dominant genre is epic poetry with strong elements of mythology and adventure.
faq.question: What is the dominant genre?

faq.answer: The main protagonist is Odysseus, the King of Ithaca.
faq.question: Who is the main protagonist of The Odyssey?

faq.answer: The story follows the Trojan War, from which Odysseus is returning home.
faq.question: What major event precedes the story of The Odyssey?

faq.answer: He faces the whims of gods, mythological monsters, and trials testing his cunning and humanity.
faq.question: What kinds of challenges does Odysseus face?

faq.answer: The Odyssey is traditionally attributed to the ancient Greek poet Homer.
faq.question: Who authored The Odyssey?

faq.answer: The central theme is the long, difficult journey home and the resilience of the human spirit against divine and monstrous obstacles.
faq.question: What is the central theme of The Odyssey?

horror.description: Some eerie mythological creatures and tense supernatural moments, but minimal horror framing.

language.description: Little to no strong language; classical epic tone with formal dialogue.

violence.description: Moderate violence including battles, monster encounters, and mythological combat, though not graphically depicted.

substance.description: Rare mention of wine or feasting, no focused substance use.

sexualContent.description: Minor romantic or mythological references to relationships, no explicit content.

emotionalIntensity.description: High emotional stakes of longing, loss, perseverance, and confrontation with fate.
